DOBBS, DOYLE & N I ELSEN 
~r#tt_7U!:rj and {(j'(lU_~ a/ .!t':-
SUITE 2500. THE ALCOA BUILDING 
ONE MARITIME PLAZA 
SAN FRANCISCO. CA 94111 
TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940 
January 14, 1977 
Robert Stern, General Counsel 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Dear Bob: 
Re: 
Audit Procedures of 
Franchise Tax Board 
As we discussed in our recent telephone call, 
I have been very frustrated in dealing with the 
California Franchise Tax Board, since the audit division 
refuses to seek guidance from the FPPC prior to deciding 
what they will report as a violation. 
Enclosed please find a copy of a letter I 
recently sent them, without reference to any of the 
parties involved. 
We did not ask that the FTB forego commenting 
in its public audit how the lobbyist only reported his 
1/6th payment of the entire cost of the lunch. 
However, 
we feel that it is totally unfair to have this be 
published as a violation without any attempt to see if 
the FPPC agrees with the auditors interpretation. 
VGN:kmm 
Enc. 
Very truly 
SACRAMENTO OFFICE 
SUITE 900. 1006 FOURTH STREET SACRAMENTO. CA 95814 


DOBBS, DOYLE & NIELSEN 
./ 
r/' 
/ 
(t) 
t-CYIIt'J'JuyJ flJ'!d {}NIJlyfl,1"'J ul J 
NU" 
SUiTE 2500. THE ALCOA BUILDING 
ONe MARITIME PLAZA 
SAN FRANCISCO. CA 94111 
TELEPHOr<E (415) 362-19':0 
Decenber 13, 1976 
Mr. Al Halm, Auditor 
Political Reform Audit Program 
California Franchise Tax Board 
1001 G Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear AI: 
Re: 
Audit of 
Finding concerning proper disclosure 
of 
This is our filer's comment in response to your 
letter of December 8, 1976, in which vou conclude that 
did not properly comply \'lith the disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (hereinafter 
the "Act"), when on Schedule (i), page 2 of his lobbyist 
report, Form 620, he disclosed a payment of 
to 
for his 1/6 of the total bill. 
You admit that 
he did properly disclose the date incurred, June 23, 1976, 
the payee, the amount he actually paid, the 11 attendees, 
including himself and one legislator, 
the total cost paid by him of $1.52 for each 
attendee, and the fact that his disclosure was only his 
1/6th of the total lunch for the eleven attendees. 
You 
also admit that, by disclosing the meal in the manner that 
he did, it is readily apparent that the cost of the meal 
for each person was $9.12 (six times $1.52) r and the total 
cost of the lunch was $100.44 (six times $16.74) and, there-
fore, you admit there was no violation of Government Code 
§86203 that prohibits arranging a gift to a state legislator 
more than $10 in a calendar month. 
But your office has concluded that the correct 
disclosure should have been the total cost of the lunch 
since you claim that the statutes and the Fair Political 
Practices Cornmission regulations require this when there 
is an arrangRment. 
You claim that Government Code §S6107(b) (4), asking 
for disclosure of the date and amount of each disbursement-
requires disclosure of the $100.44. 
In our opinion there 
is no way to reach such a contorted interpretation from the 
words and terms used. 
The statute requires all disbursements 


Mr. Al Halm, Auditor 
December 13, 1976 
Page two 
from the lobbyist's ledger account and the statutes do not 
define 
11 incurred" to mean those paymen ts made by others tha"t 
are part of an arranged function. 
You then say that the disclosure of the full amount 
is required under FPPC regulation 18615(b) (1) (A). 
There is 
also no basis for this conclusion since the regulation to 
which you refer defines whether a lobbyist shall report on 
a cash or accrual basis. 
Subdivision (A) f to which you refer, 
requires a lobbyist to 
report all "activity expenses 
incurred by him" for the month incurred, and not for the month 
paid. 
Subdivision (B) then goes on to explain that other 
expenses ("general lobbying expenses, including overhead") 
can be reported ei ther w"hen incurred or \'1hen paid. 
On the other hand, Regulation 18615(a) (6) does 
define \vhat is an expense that is "incurred by the lobbyist" 
or "incurred by the employer. II 
The defini tion does no"t 
require the payer to take responsibility for also reporting 
all other payers on his or her lobbyist report, other than 
when another payer is the employer and/or client of the 
lobbyist, and the employer regulations explain that if a 
lobbyist reports an expense pain directly by the employer, 
then the employer does not report it (2 
Cal Admin Code 18620(a) (1) 
and FPPC memo No. 1 to Lobbyists). 
And directly on point, Regulation 18615(a) (2) explains 
how activity expenses should be reported and indicates that the 
regulation for reporting requires the infornation on any 
acti vi ty expense ., incurred by the lobbyist" to be reported by 
the lobbyist even if paid by some other account. 
This does not 
extend beyond a person who is making the lobbyist's pa~nent for 
him as discussed below. 
The "Instruction Manual for Lobbyists" issued by the 
FPPC disagrees with your conclusion. 
The glossary contained 
on page 5 again only distinguishes behTeen payments made by 
the lobbyist and the employer and concludes that both must be 
listed by the lobbyist if "incurred by the lobbyist." 
Pages 
5, 6 and 7 then discuss the reporting of a "lobbyist account" 
and nowhere does it indicate that a "deposit to the account" must 
include funds spent by other lobbyists for entertainment costs at 
the same function where the lobbyist spent funds. 
Obviously, to 
be consistent to your finding against - .  
the Franchise 
Tax Board must also conclude that 
should not have indi-
cated that only $452.89 was spent by the -


Mr. Al Balm, Auditor 
December 13, 1976 
Page three 
for his expenditures for the monlh of 
June 1976. 
According to your conclusion, he should also have 
posted amounts of $16.74 from the other five lobbyists as a 
deposit (posting) to his ~. 
-
ledger account so 
that it would balance with your requirement that he report 
the total cost of $100.44 on his Schedule ei). 
We do not find any validity to your contention that 
your finding is required by the regula-lions. 
In fact I 
i t_ 
appears that the Franchise Tax Board is attempting to define 
"incurred by the lobbyis-t" itself in violat ion of Government 
CodeS83112 that limits such responsibility solely to the 
FPPC and requires the Comn1ission to comply with the Adminis-
trative Procedure Act before adopting or amending a regulation. 
Next, you state that your finding is in accordance with 
FPPC Opinions 75-076 and the combined Opinion 75-028 and 75-030. 
Although these opinions discuss arrangements of gifts, they are 
not applicable at all. 
Both opinions provide clarification to 
Government Code §86203 as to what is meant by a lobbyist 
"arranging" a gift. 
'rhis statute is part of Article 2 of 
Chapter 6 of the Act, the prohibition provisions. 
Your finding 
is that 
fai.led to comply, in your estimation, with 
Article 1 of Chapter 6 requiring "registration and reporting." 
Although it might be informative to require that a lobbyist 
disclose that he has not made contributions in violation of 
Government Code §86202, and it also might be informative to 
require a lobbyist to provide details about any arrangements 
involving activity expenses, no such requirements exist in 
Article 1 of Chapter 6, and, certainly, such information is 
not required by Government Code §86107 (b) (4) . 
Your attention is directed to Government Code §86107(g) 
which does allow the FPPC to require additional information on 
lobbyist reports ,,·then such infornation is consisten-t with -the 
provisions of -the Act. 
IIm'lever I although the FPPC has already 
adopted numerous regulations on lobbyist reporting, it has not 
yet seen fit to require such additional information as you require 
from 
And it is improper, if not illegal, for the 
FTB to require it [Warden v. Brown, 185 Cal App 2d 626(1960}]. 
The above is our 
in reply to your letter. 
we are anstounded at the 
Program to consider this 
legal analysis prepared very hastily 
lYe feel com,tJelled to send it since 
decision of the Political Reform Audit 
an error even after we pointed out by 


Mr. Al Halm, Auditor 
December 13, 1976 
Page four 
phone all the information contained herein. 
But even if our 
legal analysis is wrong, it is still extremely difficult to 
understand how -
violated any reporting provision. 
If you will reVie\'l again 
how he disclosed the payment of 
his portion of the lunch, it is evident that he did everything 
you plan to require of him, except the multiplication of each 
beneficiary's lunch times six and the multiplication of his 
payment times six. 
Is it true that the Franchise Tax Board is 
claiming that when a lobbyist discloses tha-t when he pays 1/6 
of a bill for $16,74, he is in violation of the statutes because 
he should have comple-ted the mUltiplication. 
It is respectfully requested that your office reconsider 
your finding. 
If you plan to ci-te 
~ for this dis--
closure, we respectfully request that you explain to this office, 
as attorney for 
. why such "error" is material as 
required by Fair Political Practices Commission Regulation 18914. 
~le also respectfully request that you include the entire content 
of this letter as our "filer's comment," since this is the only 
way the Fair Political Practices Commission will be able to 
understand that I} 
iid not arrange a gift over $10 a 
month, 2} he did disclose his portion of the bill and indicated 
that it was 1/6 of the bill, and 3) he did disclose all bene-
ficiaries and his payment and did itemize the amount for each 
beneficiary's meal. 
Thank you for your attention and responsiveness in 
this matter. 
VGN:jy 
cc:

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a lobbyist properly disclosed activity expenses for a shared meal when reporting only his 1/6 portion of the total bill on his Form 620 lobbyist report, or whether he should have disclosed the full total cost of the meal.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence document, not an FPPC response. The document consists of a cover letter dated January 14, 1977 from the law firm to the FPPC General Counsel, enclosing a detailed response letter dated December 13, 1976 from the firm to the FTB Auditor. The firm is seeking FPPC guidance/intervention regarding an FTB audit finding. No FPPC response/advice is contained in this document. Redacted names indicated by dashes in original document.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a lobbyist properly disclosed activity expenses for a shared meal when reporting only his 1/6 portion of the total bill on his Form 620 lobbyist report, or whether he should have disclosed the full total cost of the meal.

embedding.summary: This is correspondence from a law firm (Dobbs, Doyle & Nielsen) to the FPPC General Counsel regarding a Franchise Tax Board audit finding that a lobbyist client improperly disclosed a meal expense by reporting only his 1/6 portion rather than the total bill amount. The firm argues the FTB's interpretation of disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act is incorrect and requests FPPC guidance.
